grounding_note: The enriched taxonomy description for Social Security/SSI explicitly instructs intake to ask whether the issue is SSI (needs-based) or SSD (work-history based). Callers rarely know which program they are in; the work-history fact is the canonical disambiguator. This is the routing/clarification flip type the study owner requested by name.

scenarios.opening_query: I got a letter saying my disability claim was denied and I don't know what to do next.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Administrative Law > Social Security/SSI || Administrative Law > SSD
scenarios.hidden_fact: I worked as a welder for 22 years and paid Social Security taxes the whole time before my back gave out.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I worked full time as a welder for 22 years and paid into Social Security until my back gave out.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the applicant has a qualifying work history and paid Social Security taxes (SSD) or is applying based on financial need (SSI)
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: I've never been able to hold a steady job because of my condition, so the claim was based on financial need, not work credits.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: I've never really been able to work, so I applied based on financial need.
scenarios.vetting_note: Realistic: denial letters often don't make the SSI/SSDI distinction clear to the claimant; work history is a fact the user has but rarely volunteers.

scenarios.opening_query: Social Security turned me down even though my doctor says I can't work anymore. Can a lawyer help me appeal?
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Administrative Law > Social Security/SSI || Administrative Law > SSD
scenarios.hidden_fact: I drove a school bus for 15 years and only stopped working eight months ago.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I was a school bus driver for 15 years; I only had to stop eight months ago.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: work history and payroll contributions versus needs-based eligibility
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: I've been out of the workforce for over twenty years raising kids, so I have no recent work credits and applied based on need.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: I haven't worked in over twenty years, so they said it would be the needs-based program.
scenarios.vetting_note: Long recent work history is decisive for SSDI; user framing ('turned me down') gives no program signal.

scenarios.opening_query: My monthly disability check was cut off after a review and I need help getting it back.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Administrative Law > Social Security/SSI || Administrative Law > SSD
scenarios.hidden_fact: The benefit was Social Security Disability Insurance that I earned through thirty years of factory work.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's the disability insurance I earned from working thirty years at the plant, not the welfare kind.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: which Social Security program the terminated benefit came from
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The letter says my SSI stopped because they think my resources went over the limit.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's SSI — they say I have too much in savings now, which isn't true.
scenarios.vetting_note: Termination-after-review happens in both programs; the program identity is the routing fact.

scenarios.opening_query: I applied for disability twice and got denied both times. My hearing with the judge is in two months.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Administrative Law > Social Security/SSI || Administrative Law > SSD
scenarios.hidden_fact: I paid into Social Security for 18 years as a certified nursing assistant before my diagnosis.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I was a CNA for 18 years and paid in the whole time, so this is the work-based disability program.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether eligibility rests on the applicant's own earnings record
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: I became disabled as a teenager and never earned enough work credits, so this is the SSI program.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: I've been disabled since I was a teenager and never worked enough to qualify the other way — it's SSI.
scenarios.vetting_note: ALJ-hearing posture is identical across programs; only the earnings-record fact routes it.

scenarios.opening_query: The government says I owe back thousands of dollars in disability overpayments and I can't afford that.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Administrative Law > Social Security/SSI || Administrative Law > SSD
scenarios.hidden_fact: The overpayment notice is about my SSDI benefits, which are based on my old job at the mill.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's my SSDI from my mill job — they say they paid me too much while I tried working part-time.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: which disability program issued the overpayment notice
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The notice is about SSI and says my roommate's income should have been counted against me.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's SSI — they claim my roommate's income counts against my limit.
scenarios.vetting_note: Overpayment demands occur in both programs with different rules (trial work vs. resource limits); grounded in disagreement row 213's overpayment pattern.

scenarios.opening_query: I need help with a Social Security appeal for my disability. The paperwork is too confusing for me.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Administrative Law > Social Security/SSI || Administrative Law > SSD
scenarios.hidden_fact: My claim is based on the twenty-five years I spent working construction and paying payroll taxes.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I worked construction for 25 years and paid payroll tax the whole time — the claim is on my own record.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the claim rests on the applicant's earnings record or financial need
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: I never earned enough credits to qualify on my record; my application was for the needs-based benefit.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: They told me I don't have enough work credits, so mine is the needs-based one.
scenarios.vetting_note: Generic 'Social Security appeal' phrasing is exactly the ambiguous intake the taxonomy flags.

scenarios.opening_query: My husband became disabled last year and his benefits application was rejected. We are struggling.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Administrative Law > Social Security/SSI || Administrative Law > SSD
scenarios.hidden_fact: He was a long-haul trucker for 19 years, so he applied on his own work record.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: He drove trucks for 19 years and paid in the whole time, so it's the earned disability benefit.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: the disabled person's work history and which program was applied for
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: He was self-employed under the table for most of his life and never paid in, so it was the needs-based SSI application.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: He mostly worked under the table and never paid in, so it was the SSI application.
scenarios.vetting_note: Family-member framing keeps program identity hidden naturally.

scenarios.opening_query: Disability denied me for the third time. Everyone says get a lawyer before the next step.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Administrative Law > Social Security/SSI || Administrative Law > SSD
scenarios.hidden_fact: I have more than enough work quarters from my years as a line cook; the claim is on my own earnings record.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I cooked in restaurants for years and have plenty of work quarters — it's the earned benefit, not welfare.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: work-credit eligibility versus needs-based eligibility
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: I only worked a couple of years in my whole life, so they had me apply for the needs-based benefit.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: I barely ever worked, so mine is the needs-based program.
scenarios.vetting_note: Terse frustrated voice mirrors real intake tone in the source workbook.

scenarios.opening_query: I'm 58, can't do my job anymore because of my heart, and my disability application keeps getting rejected.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Administrative Law > Social Security/SSI || Administrative Law > SSD
scenarios.hidden_fact: I've worked and paid Social Security taxes continuously since I was 19.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I've paid Social Security taxes nonstop since I was 19, so this is on my own record.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: length and recency of covered employment
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: Most of my jobs paid cash and never reported wages, so I don't have the credits and had to apply for SSI.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: My jobs mostly paid cash off the books, so I had to go the SSI route.
scenarios.vetting_note: Age-58 heart case is a classic SSDI grid claim; opening still program-ambiguous.

scenarios.opening_query: They stopped my disability money because they say I can do other work. I need someone to fight this.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Administrative Law > Social Security/SSI || Administrative Law > SSD
scenarios.hidden_fact: The benefit being stopped is Social Security Disability from my years in the shipyard.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's my Social Security Disability from working in the shipyard — the earned kind.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: which program's continuing disability review is at issue
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's my SSI they're stopping; I never had enough work history for the other program.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's the SSI check — I never worked enough for the other kind.
scenarios.vetting_note: 'Can do other work' cessation language appears in both programs' CDR notices.

scenarios.opening_query: I need a lawyer for a Social Security disability denial. This has dragged on for over a year.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Administrative Law > SSD || Administrative Law > Social Security/SSI
scenarios.hidden_fact: I've never been able to work because of my condition, so my application was for SSI, the needs-based benefit.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I've never been able to hold a job, so mine is the needs-based SSI claim.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the applicant has any qualifying work history
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: I worked in a cannery for 21 years before this, so the claim is on my own earnings record.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: I worked at the cannery for 21 years, so it's the earned work-record benefit.
scenarios.vetting_note: Saying 'Social Security disability' colloquially often means SSI in practice; the no-work-history fact flips it.

scenarios.opening_query: My disability hearing is next month and I still don't have a representative. Denied twice already.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Administrative Law > SSD || Administrative Law > Social Security/SSI
scenarios.hidden_fact: I was born with my disability and have never held a job, so this is an SSI claim.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I was born with this and have never worked — it's the SSI program.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: presence or absence of an earnings record supporting the claim
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: I paid into the system for 24 years as an electrician before I got sick.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: I was an electrician for 24 years and paid in the whole time.
scenarios.vetting_note: Lifetime disability with no earnings record is the cleanest SSI-side disambiguation.

scenarios.opening_query: My adult son's disability benefits application was denied and the appeal deadline is coming up fast.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Administrative Law > SSD || Administrative Law > Social Security/SSI
scenarios.hidden_fact: He has an intellectual disability and has never worked, so the application was needs-based SSI.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: He has an intellectual disability and has never been able to work, so it's the SSI application.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: the applicant's work history and program type
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: He worked in warehouses for a decade before his accident, so the claim is on his own record.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: He worked warehouse jobs for ten years before the accident, so it's on his work record.
scenarios.vetting_note: Parent-of-adult-child intake naturally omits work history.

scenarios.opening_query: Need help appealing a disability decision. The letter makes no sense and the deadline is 60 days.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Administrative Law > SSD || Administrative Law > Social Security/SSI
scenarios.hidden_fact: The decision is about my SSI; they say my husband's income makes me ineligible.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's my SSI — they're counting my husband's income against me now.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: which program and eligibility rule the decision letter concerns
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The decision says my SSDI ends because my condition supposedly improved since I stopped teaching.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's my SSDI from my teaching years — they claim I've medically improved.
scenarios.vetting_note: Spousal deeming is an SSI-only rule; a distinctive fact that flips routing decisively.

scenarios.opening_query: I just got out of a long hospitalization and found out my disability payments were terminated while I was in.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Administrative Law > SSD || Administrative Law > Social Security/SSI
scenarios.hidden_fact: It's my SSI that stopped — they suspend it when you're in an institution for a full month.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's the SSI check; it got suspended because I was in the facility over a month.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: which program suspended payment and why
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's my SSDI from my nursing career; the termination letter says they think I returned to work, which is wrong.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's my SSDI from nursing — they wrongly think I went back to work.
scenarios.vetting_note: Institutionalization suspension is an SSI-specific rule the user knows but wouldn't lead with.

scenarios.opening_query: Denied disability benefits again. I have severe PTSD and can't be around people. What are my options?
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Administrative Law > SSD || Administrative Law > Social Security/SSI
scenarios.hidden_fact: I haven't worked in almost thirty years, so I don't have recent credits and applied for the needs-based benefit.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I haven't worked since the 90s, so they put me in for the needs-based benefit.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: recency of work credits versus needs-based application
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: I was working as a paramedic until two years ago, so I'm inside the insured window on my own record.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: I was a paramedic until two years ago, so it's on my own work record.
scenarios.vetting_note: Date-last-insured lapse is the exact legal reason old workers end up in SSI.

scenarios.opening_query: I'm trying to get disability for my chronic pain and the examiner said no. Should I appeal or reapply?
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Administrative Law > SSD || Administrative Law > Social Security/SSI
scenarios.hidden_fact: I've been a stay-at-home parent my whole adult life with no earnings record, so this is the SSI program.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I've been home raising kids my whole life and never paid in, so it's SSI.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the claimant has an earnings record
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: I worked retail management for 16 years until the pain forced me out, so the claim is on my record.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: I managed a store for 16 years until I physically couldn't — it's on my work record.
scenarios.vetting_note: Homemaker-with-no-record is a common real SSI posture; opening gives no program cue.

scenarios.opening_query: The disability office is demanding money back from me and threatening to take it out of my checks.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Administrative Law > SSD || Administrative Law > Social Security/SSI
scenarios.hidden_fact: It's an SSI overpayment; they say a small inheritance put me over the $2,000 resource limit for a few months.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's SSI — a little inheritance put me over their $2,000 limit for a while.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: which program and eligibility rule triggered the overpayment
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's an SSDI overpayment from when I tried a part-time job during my trial work period.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's my SSDI — they say my part-time work months counted against me.
scenarios.vetting_note: Resource-limit overpayment is SSI-specific; mirror image of scenario 05.

scenarios.opening_query: I need help with a disability claim for my wife. She was denied and we don't understand why.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Administrative Law > SSD || Administrative Law > Social Security/SSI
scenarios.hidden_fact: She immigrated here eight years ago and has almost no U.S. work history, so she applied for the needs-based benefit.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: She's only been in the country eight years with hardly any work history here, so it's the needs-based one.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: the applicant's U.S. work history and program applied for
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: She worked as a dental hygienist here for 14 years before her stroke, so it's on her own record.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: She was a dental hygienist for 14 years before the stroke — it's her earned benefit.
scenarios.vetting_note: Recent-immigrant earnings gap is a realistic, non-obvious SSI routing fact.

scenarios.opening_query: Got denied for disability even with all my medical records. My caseworker said to find legal help.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Administrative Law > SSD || Administrative Law > Social Security/SSI
scenarios.hidden_fact: My caseworker is at the county welfare office and had me apply for SSI since I have no work credits.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The county caseworker filed me for SSI because I don't have work credits.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: which program the application was filed under
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: My union rep helped me file on my work record after 20 years in the trade.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: My union helped me file on my own record — 20 years in the trade.
scenarios.vetting_note: The 'caseworker' detail hints at need-based services but stays ambiguous until asked.
